title: Glossary
text:
A growing list of key ESS terms. Keep definitions short and consistent. (The search box in the top bar searches this page too.)








Term
Definition
Topic




Environmental value system (EVS)
A worldview shaping how an individual or group perceives and acts on the environment.
1.1


Sustainability
Use of resources at a rate that allows natural regeneration and avoids long-term damage.
1.3


Natural capital
Natural resources that produce a flow of goods or services over time.
1.3


Carrying capacity
The maximum population an environment can support indefinitely.
2.1


Biodiversity
The variety of life: genetic, species, and habitat diversity.
3.1



Add a row whenever you introduce a new term on a topic page.

title: 1.1 Perspectives
text:
NoteWhat this topic covers




An environmental value system (EVS) is a worldview that shapes how a person or society perceives and acts towards the environment.
An EVS can be modelled as a system: inputs (education, culture, religion, media, personal experience) lead to outputs (decisions, behaviours, policies).
Perspectives sit on a spectrum from ecocentric → anthropocentric → technocentric.

title: 1.1 Perspectives
text:
Key concepts
A perspective is the lens through which someone interprets environmental issues. Two people can look at the same forest and see completely different things — a sacred ecosystem, a carbon store, a source of timber, or a site for development. Those differences come from their environmental value system.
An EVS is useful because it behaves like a system. Information and experiences go in; values, decisions, and actions come out. Because the inputs differ from person to person and culture to culture, the outputs — how we treat the environment — differ too.

The spectrum of perspectives





flowchart LR
    A["Ecocentric<br/>nature-centred<br/>minimal intervention"]
    B["Anthropocentric<br/>people-centred<br/>manage & regulate"]
    C["Technocentric<br/>technology-centred<br/>solve with innovation"]
    A --> B --> C
    style A fill:#cfe9d8,stroke:#2e7d52
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#caa436
    style C fill:#d6e4f0,stroke:#3a6ea5







Ecocentric — puts nature at the centre; values ecological balance and prefers working with natural systems over controlling them.
Anthropocentric — puts people at the centre; sees the environment as something to be managed sustainably for human benefit, often through regulation and taxes.
Technocentric — trusts science and technology to solve environmental problems and to keep raising living standards.

Most real people and societies sit somewhere along this spectrum rather than at one extreme, and a person’s position can shift depending on the issue.


An EVS as a system





flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Education]
        I2[Culture & religion]
        I3[Media]
        I4[Personal experience]
    end
    EVS([Environmental<br/>Value System])
    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Decisions]
        O2[Behaviours]
        O3[Policies]
    end
    I1 --> EVS
    I2 --> EVS
    I3 --> EVS
    I4 --> EVS
    EVS --> O1
    EVS --> O2
    EVS --> O3

title: 1.1 Perspectives
text:
Key terms







Term
Meaning




Environmental value system (EVS)
A worldview shaping how an individual or group perceives and acts on the environment.


Ecocentric
A nature-centred perspective favouring minimal human interference.


Anthropocentric
A people-centred perspective focused on managing the environment for human use.


Technocentric
A perspective that relies on technology and innovation to solve environmental problems.


Perspective
The particular way a person or group interprets an environmental issue.

title: 1.1 Perspectives
text:
Check yourself






TipQuick questions






Define environmental value system in your own words.
Place each on the spectrum: a deep-ecology activist, a government economist, a geo-engineering start-up founder.
Explain how two different inputs could lead two people to opposite views on building a dam.
To what extent does a person’s EVS stay fixed over their lifetime?

title: Case Studies
text: ESS rewards named, specific examples. This is where you collect them — one short, structured entry per case, tagged by topic so they’re easy to find at revision time.

title: Case Studies
section: Template for a new case study
text:
Template for a new case study
Copy this block for each case:

Name of the case (place + year)
Topic links: e.g. 3.3 Conservation, 8.1 Human populations What happened: one or two sentences Causes: bullet points Impacts: environmental, social, economic Management / response: what was done, and how well it worked

title: Case Studies
text:
Example

Costa Rica Payments for Ecosystem Services (1997– )
Topic links: 3.3 Conservation & regeneration, 7.1 Natural resources What happened: Costa Rica began paying landowners to conserve and restore forest. Causes: High deforestation rates in the late 20th century. Impacts: Forest cover recovered substantially; ecotourism grew. Management / response: A national fund financed by fuel taxes and international support.

(Check the figures and details against current sources before publishing.)

title: About
text:
ESSential Resources is a free, open project sharing notes, diagrams, case studies, and data for Environmental Systems & Societies and environmental education more broadly.
Everything is free to read and share under a Creative Commons licence.
Add your name, your story, and a contact email here.

title: ESSential Resources
text:
Welcome! This site is a growing, free collection of notes, diagrams, case studies, and data for Environmental Systems & Societies (ESS) and for anyone curious about how the natural world and human society fit together.
Everything here is free to read and share. Pick a topic below, or browse the full topic list.

title: ESSential Resources
text:
What you’ll find here

Topic notes that follow the syllabus, each laid out the same way so they’re easy to revise from.
A glossary of the key terms ESS loves to test.
A case-study library of named, specific examples.
Tools & data — interactive charts and calculators (the fun part).


This is an early draft built as a starting point. Content should always be checked against the official subject guide before relying on it for an exam.

title: Tools & Data
text:
This is where the platform earns its keep — the interactive, data-driven pieces that a simple page builder can’t do. Planned tools:

Live climate charts — atmospheric CO₂, global temperature anomaly, pulled from open data so they stay current.
Diversity-index calculator — enter species counts, get Simpson’s index.
Ecological-footprint estimator — a short questionnaire with a result.
Worked statistics examples — with downloadable CSV data to try yourself.

When you’re ready, these can be built with R or Python code blocks (Quarto runs both), or with small interactive widgets. Ask and we’ll build the first one together.
